statement: Ireland provides a different model of EU opt-outs relevant to Iceland. Ireland is not part of the Schengen Area (maintaining its own border controls), has opt-ins rather than opt-outs for justice and home affairs measures (choosing case by case whether to participate), and has a constitutional protocol guaranteeing neutrality in EU defence policy (the Lisbon Treaty's Irish Protocol). Ireland also secured a protocol confirming that EU law does not affect Ireland's constitutional provisions on abortion. These precedents suggest that sovereignty-sensitive issues can be accommodated within EU membership through protocols and declarations in accession treaties.
source_name: Treaty of Lisbon — Protocol on the concerns of the Irish people; Protocol 21 on UK and Ireland (JHA opt-in)
caveats: Ireland's opt-outs were secured at different historical moments (Maastricht, Amsterdam, Lisbon) and partly reflect the leverage of referendum rejections. Iceland would need to negotiate comparable protections during accession, without the same leverage. The EU's acquis has expanded significantly since Ireland's accession in 1973.

related_entries.statement: Denmark secured four opt-outs from the Maastricht Treaty in 1992 following the initial 'No' referendum: the euro, defence, justice and home affairs, and EU citizenship (the latter was later rendered moot by the Amsterdam Treaty). These opt-outs remain in force and provide a precedent for Iceland. In June 2022, Denmark voted in a referendum to abolish its defence opt-out, joining the EU's Common Security and Defence Policy. The remaining opt-outs — particularly on the euro and justice — demonstrate that EU membership does not require full integration. However, opt-outs carry costs: Denmark is excluded from eurozone governance and some JHA cooperation, reducing its influence in these areas.

Iceland implements approximately 75% of EU single market legislation through the EEA Agreement without having any voting rights in the EU institutions that draft and adopt these laws. The EEA Joint Committee, where Iceland has a formal seat, operates by consensus but in practice rubber-stamps EU legislation — Iceland has never exercised its Article 102 'right of reservation' to veto an EEA-relevant act. The EFTA pillar's consultative role is limited to providing input before legislation is finalised, with no amendment power. This arrangement has been described as a 'fax democracy' by Norwegian critics — Iceland receives the laws by fax (metaphorically) and implements them without meaningful influence over their content.

citations.report_title: Engin fordæmi fyrir leið Íslands í átt að ESB

claims.claim_text: Mörg ESB-ríki hafa fengið sérlausnir og undanþágur um málefni sem þeim skipta máli.
RÚV greindi frá því síðastliðinn föstudag að engar varanlegar undanþágur séu í boði við ESB-aðild.
Ekki sé lengur hægt að fá undanþágur frá skilmálum ESB-aðildar.
Stefan Füle, þáverandi stækkunarstjóri ESB, hafi sagt á blaðamannafundi með Össuri Skarphéðinssyni að engar varanlegar undanþágur séu frá regluverki ESB.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Tollvernd er hluti af sameiginlegri löggjöf Evrópusambandsins og því var óraunhæft fyrir Ísland að fara fram á að halda henni.
Ísland fór fram á sérlausn innan sameiginlegrar sjávarútvegsstefnu ESB.
